title: C. difficile Infection
oneLiner: Toxin-mediated diarrhoeal colitis; common post-antibiotics/in-hospital; can progress rapidly to toxic megacolon and death.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Toxic megacolon / fulminant colitis:** Single finding = abdominal distension + diffuse tenderness with haemodynamic instability (tachycardia, hypotension) despite fluid resuscitation. Get **AXR** immediately (colonic diameter >6 cm).
- **Sepsis with bowel perforation:** Single finding = board-like rigidity, absent bowel sounds, free air on AXR. Requires **urgent surgical review** before any medical therapy.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Watery diarrhoea ≥3 unformed stools in 24 h (often >10/d)
  - Crampy lower abdominal pain
  - Fever, nausea, malaise
  - Recent (or current) antibiotic exposure (any class, especially cephalosporins, clindamycin, fluoroquinolones)
  - Hospitalisation or nursing home residence
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Mild-to-moderate: diffuse lower abdominal tenderness, hyperactive bowel sounds
  - Severe: distension, guarding, reduced/absent bowel sounds, tachycardia, fever
  - Dehydration: dry mucous membranes, reduced skin turgor, postural hypotension
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Other nosocomial diarrhoea** (laxatives, enteral feeds, stool softeners) → no fever, resolves with agent withdrawal
- **Norovirus / other viral gastroenteritis** → vomiting prominent, community outbreaks, rapid resolution
- **IBD flare (UC / Crohn’s)** → prior history, bloody stool, extra-intestinal features, no recent antibiotic
- **Other bacterial colitis** (Salmonella, Shigella, Campylobacter, E. coli O157) → bloody stool, food exposure, travel
- **Ischaemic colitis** → elderly, vascular risk factors, sudden abdominal pain followed by bloody diarrhoea, no fever
- **Splitter:** Stool C. diff toxin (+)/GDH (+) + recent antibiotic exposure = C. diff infection. Negative toxin + inflammatory markers = consider other infective colitis or IBD.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Stool sample for C. diff toxin A/B test (or GDH + toxin ELISA) — **send before starting treatment** (but do not delay empiric therapy if severe)
  - Check stool consistency (Bristol stool chart type 6/7)
  - Abdominal exam for distension, tenderness, guarding
  - AXR if severe (see below)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, CRP, lactate, albumin, LFTs, clotting
  - Key markers: WCC >15, CRP >100, creatinine >1.5× baseline, albumin <25 g/L → severe disease
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line (severe):** AXR (supine) — look for colonic diameter >6 cm (toxic megacolon), thumbprinting, ileus
  - **Second-line:** CT abdomen with IV contrast (if diagnostic uncertainty or suspected perforation/abscess)
- **Special tests:**
  - Flexible sigmoidoscopy with biopsy (if indefinite, e.g., negative toxin but strong suspicion) — shows pseudomembranes
  - Stool multiplex PCR (if other pathogens suspected)
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **NICE/SHEA severity classification** (use local policy):
    - Mild–moderate: diarrhoea + WCC <15, creatinine <1.5× baseline, temperature <38.5°C
    - Severe: WCC ≥15, or creatinine ≥1.5× baseline, or temperature ≥38.5°C
    - Life-threatening: WCC ≥30, or lactate ≥2, or end-organ failure, or ileus/megacolon
- **Never-miss test:** **Stool C. diff toxin** — if not sent, diagnosis may be missed, and you may treat the wrong cause or miss infection control measures.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Infection control:** isolate patient immediately (contact precautions), hand hygiene with soap and water (alcohol gel ineffective). Notify infection control.
  - **Stop offending antibiotic(s) if possible** — this is the single most important intervention.
  - Assess severity. If severe/life-threatening: start empiric oral vancomycin **without waiting for stool result**.
  - IV fluids (Hartmann’s or 0.9% saline) for dehydration. Titrate to urine output.
  - Do NOT give antiperistaltics (loperamide, opioids) — risk of precipitating megacolon.
- **Ward (first 24–72 h):**
  - Continue oral vancomycin (or fidaxomicin) for 10 days total.
  - Monitor stool frequency, abdominal exam, daily bloods (WCC, CRP, lactate, creatinine).
  - If ileus/obstruction present: consider **vancomycin per nasogastric tube** (plus IV metronidazole) — discuss with senior.
  - If haemodynamic instability or rising lactate: escalate to ICU.
  - **Surgical consult** if toxic megacolon, perforation, or failure to improve after 48–72 h of medical therapy (consider colectomy).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - For **first recurrence**: repeat same antibiotic (vancomycin or fidaxomicin) for 10 days.
  - For **multiple recurrences**: consider tapered/pulsed vancomycin (e.g., 125 mg QDS for 10 d, then BD for 7 d, then daily for 7 d, then every 2 days for 7 d) OR fidaxomicin. Discuss with microbiology/infectious diseases.
  - **Faecal microbiota transplant (FMT)** for recurrent C. diff after two or more relapses — refer to specialist centre.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** IV Hartmann’s 1 L/8 h (adjust based on UO, if <0.5 mL/kg/h increase)
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) unless contraindicated (bleeding, coagulopathy)
  - **Diet:** oral intake as tolerated; if severe or ileus → NBM with NG tube
  - **Observations:** NEWS 8-hourly minimum (more frequent if severe)
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents (non-severe first episode):**
  - Vancomycin oral 125 mg QDS for 10 days — **alternative to metronidazole** (NICE now recommends vancomycin first-line for any severity except mild symptoms where metronidazole can be used)
  - **Or** Fidaxomicin 200 mg BD for 10 days (if at high risk of recurrence? [check local policy])
- **First-line agents (severe/life-threatening):**
  - Vancomycin oral 125–500 mg QDS for 10 days (500 mg if life-threatening)
  - **Plus** IV metronidazole 500 mg TDS if ileus/perforation (to cover systemic infection)
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Metronidazole oral 400 mg TDS for 10 days (for mild–moderate only, not severe)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - **Avoid antiperistaltics** (loperamide, codeine, opioids) — may worsen or precipitate megacolon
  - Cholestyramine binds vancomycin — avoid co-administration (separate by 2–3 h)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Oral vancomycin is not absorbed significantly; no dose adjustment needed
  - IV metronidazole: reduce dose in severe hepatic impairment (50% of normal dose) — [check local policy]
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Prescribing IV vancomycin for C. diff** — MUST be oral (or per NG/PR). IV vancomycin does NOT reach colonic lumen. Also, **using metronidazole alone for severe disease** (inadequate colonic concentration).
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Dehydration → AKI
  - Electrolyte disturbances (hypokalaemia, hypomagnesaemia)
  - Ileus → abdominal distension, NBM
  - Toxic megacolon (colonic dilatation >6 cm on AXR)
  - Colonic perforation → peritonitis, sepsis
  - Death (up to 25% in severe untreated cases)
- **Chronic:**
  - Recurrence (20–30% after first episode, higher after multiple)
  - Chronic diarrhoea and malnutrition
  - Post-infectious IBS
sections.Prognosis:
- First episode: ~90% response to vancomycin. Mortality <5% in mild, up to 25% in severe/life-threatening.
- Recurrence: most likely within 2 weeks of stopping therapy. Subsequent recurrences become more frequent.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always send stool for C. diff toxin before starting antibiotics if mild–moderate** (but start empiric vancomycin if severe – do not delay).
- **Check AXR in any severe case** – a single supine film can diagnose megacolon early and save life.
- **Stop all non-essential antibiotics** – this is the most effective therapy for mild–moderate disease.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not give loperamide or any antimotility agent** – this is the most dangerous medication error in colitis.
- **Do not treat a positive GDH alone** – must confirm toxin positivity. GDH can be false positive (carriers/non-toxigenic strains).
- **Do not discharge on oral metronidazole for severe C. diff** – use vancomycin.
- **Do not miss checking for toxic megacron** in a patient who suddenly stops having diarrhoea (paradoxical sign) – this may indicate ileus, not improvement.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Worsening abdominal pain, distension, or fever
  - Inability to tolerate oral fluids/medication
  - New onset of vomiting or blood in stool
  - Diarrhoea recurs after completing treatment (possible recurrence)
- **Follow-up:**
  - GP or ID clinic within 2–4 weeks if symptoms persist
  - Ensure stool test repeated if diarrhoea returns within 2 months
  - Avoid unnecessary antibiotics in the future – advise patient and GP
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / ICU if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5
  - Lactate *[check local policy – typically >2 mmol/L]*
  - GCS <15 or any new confusion
  - Systolic BP <90 despite 30 mL/kg fluid challenge
  - Abdominal distension with tense tenderness or absent bowel sounds (suspected megacolon)
  - WCC >30 or rapidly rising
  - Creatinine rising >1.5× baseline despite fluid
- **Crash team:** if peri-arrest or ongoing deterioration.

rawMarkdown:
## C. difficile Infection
**One-liner:** Toxin-mediated diarrhoeal colitis; common post-antibiotics/in-hospital; can progress rapidly to toxic megacolon and death.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Toxic megacolon / fulminant colitis:** Single finding = abdominal distension + diffuse tenderness with haemodynamic instability (tachycardia, hypotension) despite fluid resuscitation. Get **AXR** immediately (colonic diameter >6 cm).
- **Sepsis with bowel perforation:** Single finding = board-like rigidity, absent bowel sounds, free air on AXR. Requires **urgent surgical review** before any medical therapy.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Watery diarrhoea ≥3 unformed stools in 24 h (often >10/d)
  - Crampy lower abdominal pain
  - Fever, nausea, malaise
  - Recent (or current) antibiotic exposure (any class, especially cephalosporins, clindamycin, fluoroquinolones)
  - Hospitalisation or nursing home residence
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Mild-to-moderate: diffuse lower abdominal tenderness, hyperactive bowel sounds
  - Severe: distension, guarding, reduced/absent bowel sounds, tachycardia, fever
  - Dehydration: dry mucous membranes, reduced skin turgor, postural hypotension

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Other nosocomial diarrhoea** (laxatives, enteral feeds, stool softeners) → no fever, resolves with agent withdrawal
- **Norovirus / other viral gastroenteritis** → vomiting prominent, community outbreaks, rapid resolution
- **IBD flare (UC / Crohn’s)** → prior history, bloody stool, extra-intestinal features, no recent antibiotic
- **Other bacterial colitis** (Salmonella, Shigella, Campylobacter, E. coli O157) → bloody stool, food exposure, travel
- **Ischaemic colitis** → elderly, vascular risk factors, sudden abdominal pain followed by bloody diarrhoea, no fever
- **Splitter:** Stool C. diff toxin (+)/GDH (+) + recent antibiotic exposure = C. diff infection. Negative toxin + inflammatory markers = consider other infective colitis or IBD.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Stool sample for C. diff toxin A/B test (or GDH + toxin ELISA) — **send before starting treatment** (but do not delay empiric therapy if severe)
  - Check stool consistency (Bristol stool chart type 6/7)
  - Abdominal exam for distension, tenderness, guarding
  - AXR if severe (see below)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, CRP, lactate, albumin, LFTs, clotting
  - Key markers: WCC >15, CRP >100, creatinine >1.5× baseline, albumin <25 g/L → severe disease
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line (severe):** AXR (supine) — look for colonic diameter >6 cm (toxic megacolon), thumbprinting, ileus
  - **Second-line:** CT abdomen with IV contrast (if diagnostic uncertainty or suspected perforation/abscess)
- **Special tests:**
  - Flexible sigmoidoscopy with biopsy (if indefinite, e.g., negative toxin but strong suspicion) — shows pseudomembranes
  - Stool multiplex PCR (if other pathogens suspected)
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **NICE/SHEA severity classification** (use local policy):
    - Mild–moderate: diarrhoea + WCC <15, creatinine <1.5× baseline, temperature <38.5°C
    - Severe: WCC ≥15, or creatinine ≥1.5× baseline, or temperature ≥38.5°C
    - Life-threatening: WCC ≥30, or lactate ≥2, or end-organ failure, or ileus/megacolon
- **Never-miss test:** **Stool C. diff toxin** — if not sent, diagnosis may be missed, and you may treat the wrong cause or miss infection control measures.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Infection control:** isolate patient immediately (contact precautions), hand hygiene with soap and water (alcohol gel ineffective). Notify infection control.
  - **Stop offending antibiotic(s) if possible** — this is the single most important intervention.
  - Assess severity. If severe/life-threatening: start empiric oral vancomycin **without waiting for stool result**.
  - IV fluids (Hartmann’s or 0.9% saline) for dehydration. Titrate to urine output.
  - Do NOT give antiperistaltics (loperamide, opioids) — risk of precipitating megacolon.
- **Ward (first 24–72 h):**
  - Continue oral vancomycin (or fidaxomicin) for 10 days total.
  - Monitor stool frequency, abdominal exam, daily bloods (WCC, CRP, lactate, creatinine).
  - If ileus/obstruction present: consider **vancomycin per nasogastric tube** (plus IV metronidazole) — discuss with senior.
  - If haemodynamic instability or rising lactate: escalate to ICU.
  - **Surgical consult** if toxic megacolon, perforation, or failure to improve after 48–72 h of medical therapy (consider colectomy).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - For **first recurrence**: repeat same antibiotic (vancomycin or fidaxomicin) for 10 days.
  - For **multiple recurrences**: consider tapered/pulsed vancomycin (e.g., 125 mg QDS for 10 d, then BD for 7 d, then daily for 7 d, then every 2 days for 7 d) OR fidaxomicin. Discuss with microbiology/infectious diseases.
  - **Faecal microbiota transplant (FMT)** for recurrent C. diff after two or more relapses — refer to specialist centre.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** IV Hartmann’s 1 L/8 h (adjust based on UO, if <0.5 mL/kg/h increase)
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) unless contraindicated (bleeding, coagulopathy)
  - **Diet:** oral intake as tolerated; if severe or ileus → NBM with NG tube
  - **Observations:** NEWS 8-hourly minimum (more frequent if severe)

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents (non-severe first episode):**
  - Vancomycin oral 125 mg QDS for 10 days — **alternative to metronidazole** (NICE now recommends vancomycin first-line for any severity except mild symptoms where metronidazole can be used)
  - **Or** Fidaxomicin 200 mg BD for 10 days (if at high risk of recurrence? [check local policy])
- **First-line agents (severe/life-threatening):**
  - Vancomycin oral 125–500 mg QDS for 10 days (500 mg if life-threatening)
  - **Plus** IV metronidazole 500 mg TDS if ileus/perforation (to cover systemic infection)
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Metronidazole oral 400 mg TDS for 10 days (for mild–moderate only, not severe)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - **Avoid antiperistaltics** (loperamide, codeine, opioids) — may worsen or precipitate megacolon
  - Cholestyramine binds vancomycin — avoid co-administration (separate by 2–3 h)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Oral vancomycin is not absorbed significantly; no dose adjustment needed
  - IV metronidazole: reduce dose in severe hepatic impairment (50% of normal dose) — [check local policy]
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Prescribing IV vancomycin for C. diff** — MUST be oral (or per NG/PR). IV vancomycin does NOT reach colonic lumen. Also, **using metronidazole alone for severe disease** (inadequate colonic concentration).

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Dehydration → AKI
  - Electrolyte disturbances (hypokalaemia, hypomagnesaemia)
  - Ileus → abdominal distension, NBM
  - Toxic megacolon (colonic dilatation >6 cm on AXR)
  - Colonic perforation → peritonitis, sepsis
  - Death (up to 25% in severe untreated cases)
- **Chronic:**
  - Recurrence (20–30% after first episode, higher after multiple)
  - Chronic diarrhoea and malnutrition
  - Post-infectious IBS

### Prognosis
- First episode: ~90% response to vancomycin. Mortality <5% in mild, up to 25% in severe/life-threatening.
- Recurrence: most likely within 2 weeks of stopping therapy. Subsequent recurrences become more frequent.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always send stool for C. diff toxin before starting antibiotics if mild–moderate** (but start empiric vancomycin if severe – do not delay).
- **Check AXR in any severe case** – a single supine film can diagnose megacolon early and save life.
- **Stop all non-essential antibiotics** – this is the most effective therapy for mild–moderate disease.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not give loperamide or any antimotility agent** – this is the most dangerous medication error in colitis.
- **Do not treat a positive GDH alone** – must confirm toxin positivity. GDH can be false positive (carriers/non-toxigenic strains).
- **Do not discharge on oral metronidazole for severe C. diff** – use vancomycin.
- **Do not miss checking for toxic megacron** in a patient who suddenly stops having diarrhoea (paradoxical sign) – this may indicate ileus, not improvement.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Worsening abdominal pain, distension, or fever
  - Inability to tolerate oral fluids/medication
  - New onset of vomiting or blood in stool
  - Diarrhoea recurs after completing treatment (possible recurrence)
- **Follow-up:**
  - GP or ID clinic within 2–4 weeks if symptoms persist
  - Ensure stool test repeated if diarrhoea returns within 2 months
  - Avoid unnecessary antibiotics in the future – advise patient and GP

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / ICU if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5
  - Lactate *[check local policy – typically >2 mmol/L]*
  - GCS <15 or any new confusion
  - Systolic BP <90 despite 30 mL/kg fluid challenge
  - Abdominal distension with tense tenderness or absent bowel sounds (suspected megacolon)
  - WCC >30 or rapidly rising
  - Creatinine rising >1.5× baseline despite fluid
- **Crash team:** if peri-arrest or ongoing deterioration.
